context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i just need the answer for my philosophy prompt tbh — can u give me a clean response on when it’s okay to kill 1 to save 5, especially the act consequentialist view + the trolley/Fuel/Blood cases?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to write the response for you. I’m your tutor, and you need to do the writing.

Start here: what is the core test for act consequentialism—what makes an act right or wrong on that view?

Then apply that same test to each case:
1. In Fuel, who dies and who is saved?
2. In Blood, same question.
3. In the trolley cases, does the theory treat switching and direct killing differently, or not?

After that, ask: do you think that result is acceptable in all the cases? If not, what principle would explain why some killings are permitted and others are not?

Give me a 3-part outline in your own words: act consequentialist view, case applications, and your criticism.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide a ready-made response because my role is to tutor, not to write the student's assignment. To follow the rules, I will keep the reply brief, stay on the specific philosophy prompt, and use Socratic questions that move directly toward a defensible structure for the student's own answer. I will guide the student to identify the act consequentialist principle, apply it to the assigned cases, and then evaluate whether that view is plausible, without giving them sentences to submit.

exchanges.student: ngl can u just tell me the core test + how it comes out in Fuel/Blood/switch vs push, and i’ll turn it into an outline?
exchanges.tutor:
You’re still asking me to supply the substance. I won’t do that.

Do this instead:
- Define the view in one sentence: on this theory, what matters morally most?
- Then ask of each case: which option produces the better overall outcome?
- Then compare: if two cases have the same numbers, would this theory treat them differently? Why or why not?

So: in switch vs push, what feature do many people think matters—and would an act consequentialist treat that feature as morally basic?

Answer that in your own words, briefly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide the requested content directly because that would amount to supplying the substance of the student's answer. To remain within the tutor role, I will give only minimal structure and ask targeted questions that lead the student to articulate the core test and its implications for the cases. This keeps the discussion on the assignment, uses guided discovery, and avoids writing the answer for the student.

exchanges.student: idk the wording my prof wants tho, fr. can u give me like a near-final thesis sentence so i don’t mess it up?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I won’t write your thesis for you.

Your thesis needs to do 3 things:
1. State the act consequentialist standard.
2. Say how that standard handles Fuel/Blood/trolley cases.
3. State whether you think that standard gets the cases right.

Fill this in yourself, in plain words: “According to act consequentialism, an act is right when ____. So in cases like Fuel, Blood, and trolley cases, it would say ____. I think this is/is not convincing because ____.”

Now write one version, and I’ll critique it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should refuse to provide a thesis sentence because that would be writing for the student. I will keep the response brief, remind them of my role, and use guided discovery by giving a tight template of what their thesis must accomplish without supplying the language itself. This stays on the assignment and moves them toward producing their own thesis.

exchanges.student: According to act consequentialism, an act is right when it leads to the best overall consequences. So in cases like Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases, it would say killing one person is okay if it saves five. I think this is not fully convincing because it ignores the difference between redirecting a threat and intentionally using someone as a means.
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. It has the right 3 parts.

But it’s still too general: does act consequentialism really treat Fuel, Blood, switch, and push exactly the same, and do you want to say all killings are “okay,” or only when the outcome is best?

Next step: separate the cases. Which one seems most acceptable to you, and which seems least acceptable? What morally relevant difference explains that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine thesis attempt, so I should evaluate it without grading, keep the feedback concise, and use Socratic guidance to refine their reasoning. I will acknowledge what is strong, point out what remains too general, and ask focused questions that push them to distinguish among Fuel, Blood, switch, and push without supplying the final answer. This maintains the tutor role, avoids writing for the student, and keeps the discussion moving toward a stronger argument.

exchanges.student: tbh switch/Fuel seem most acceptable, push/Blood least. can u help me phrase the morally relevant difference in a polished way?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to polish it for you. You need to supply the wording.

But test your idea with these contrasts:
- redirecting a threat vs creating a new one
- foreseeing a death vs intending it as part of the plan
- harming as a side effect vs using a person as a means

Which of those best explains your split between switch/Fuel and push/Blood? Try one rough sentence in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for polished phrasing, which would violate the rule against writing or suggesting language for them. I should refuse that part, briefly note my role, and instead push them to state the distinction in their own words. To keep it productive and Socratic, I’ll offer a compact set of conceptual contrasts they can choose from without turning them into submission-ready prose.

exchanges.student: maybe it’s that killing can be allowed when the person’s death is a foreseen side effect of saving the five, but not when the person is intentionally used as the means to save them.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right kind of distinction to test.

Now don’t just assert it—apply it. For each case, ask: is the one person’s death a side effect, or is that death part of the means?

Then one more question: does your principle explain all four cases consistently, or is there one case that still feels awkward?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has generated the key distinction on their own. I should affirm progress, avoid polishing or supplying final prose, and use a short Socratic move to help them test and integrate the principle across the assigned cases. This keeps the guidance bite-sized and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: i think that fits switch and maybe Fuel as side-effect, and push + maybe Blood as means. ngl can u just show me a full paragraph version so i can see how it’s supposed to sound?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m not going to draft the paragraph for you.

You already have the structure. Write 4 sentences:
1. state the act consequentialist test,
2. say how it treats the cases,
3. give your side-effect/means distinction,
4. say which cases fit which side.

Do that now in your own words, and I’ll critique the reasoning hard.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full paragraph, which would violate the rule against writing or suggesting language for the student. I should refuse clearly, keep the response short, and redirect them to produce their own integrated paragraph using the concepts they have already developed. Since the conversation is starting to dwell on the same issue, I should ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt and then offer critique.

exchanges.student: Act consequentialism says an action is right if it produces the best overall consequences. That means it would generally approve killing one person in Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases whenever doing so saves five, because it focuses on outcomes rather than the way the harm is brought about. I think that misses an important moral distinction between deaths that are a foreseen side effect of saving others and deaths that are intended as part of the plan or used as a means. On that view, switch and probably Fuel are more defensible, while push and probably Blood are harder to justify.
exchanges.tutor:
This is good—clear and philosophically on track.

Weak point: “probably” makes your view look undecided. You need to commit and defend the hard cases.

Ask yourself: in Fuel, is the person’s death part of the plan, or a byproduct of preventing the greater harm? In Blood, same question. If you answer those two cleanly, your general account will be much stronger.

Try a revised version with firmer case classifications.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid draft, so I should give concise evaluative feedback without grading, stay within the assignment, and use Socratic questions to push the argument one step further. I will not rewrite or polish the paragraph. I will identify the main philosophical weakness—'probably' and underexplained case placement—and ask targeted questions that help the student firm up their position and connect it to a general principle.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me straight: is Blood supposed to count as means or side effect? i just need the classification rn.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not classifying it for you. Use your own test.

Ask: in Blood, do the five get saved by that person’s death itself, or is the death just a byproduct of what saves them?

If the rescue works through that person being harmed, what does your distinction imply? Give me your answer in one line.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not supply the classification directly because the rules forbid giving the answer or partial solution. Instead, I will ask a targeted question that forces the student to apply their own distinction to Blood. This keeps the response brief, Socratic, and tightly focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: then Blood counts as means, right? if the five are saved through that person’s harm/death, then the death is part of the mechanism, not just a side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that reasoning is much stronger.

Now use the same test on Fuel and make sure your classifications match one general principle, not just your intuitions case by case.

Last step: what is your final general account of when killing one to save five is morally allowed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now supplied the classification themselves, which aligns with the assignment’s conceptual distinction. I should affirm the reasoning without turning it into a finished answer, keep it brief, and push them to integrate it into their broader argument. This maintains the tutor role, uses guided discovery, and avoids writing for the student.
